text:
The butter-side-down phenomenon is not an accident. It is, in fact, a conspiracy.

Here is what actually happens. When you butter your toast, the butter absorbs the toast's confidence. The toast, now emboldened by fat, believes it can fly. It tips off the counter convinced of its own aerodynamic potential. It is wrong.

During the fall, the toast attempts to correct itself mid-air. This is hubris. Physics does not negotiate with carbohydrates. The toast rotates approximately half a turn before hitting the floor, which is exactly enough to land butter-side down. If the ceiling were higher, the toast would complete a full rotation and land butter-side up. The universe measured your ceiling specifically to prevent this.

The real culprit is gravity, which has had millions of years to develop a personality, and that personality is petty. Gravity applies slightly more force to the buttered side because butter is denser and more delicious. Gravity prefers things to be ruined. This is consistent with gravity's entire track record.

There is also a psychological component. The moment you see the toast falling, your brain calculates the outcome and generates dread. That dread is heavy. It pulls the buttered side earthward. You are partially responsible.

Some researchers suggest the true cause is that toast always falls from table height, which is roughly one meter, and one meter is precisely enough for a half rotation at toast's typical drag coefficient. These researchers are correct but boring.

The simplest explanation is that the universe is efficient and fair, and fairness means that the nicely buttered side must face consequences. The floor is the consequence. This is physics, philosophy, and breakfast all at once.

Clean it up and try again.
